pattern: An in-order walk of a search tree already produces its values in sorted order, so the combined answer is just a standard merge of the two trees' in-order sequences.
hints: Collect each tree's values via an in-order traversal; each resulting list is already sorted ascending.
Merge the two sorted lists the same way you would merge two sorted arrays, always taking the smaller of the two current heads.
Once one list runs out, append whatever remains of the other list unchanged.

edge_inputs.description: two small search trees with interleaved values
two search trees that share duplicate values
first tree empty, second has one node
identical single value in both trees
single-node trees at opposite ends of the value range

stress.description: two random binary search trees from shuffled permutations, moderate size

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def getAllElements(self, root1, root2):
        def inorder(node, acc):
            if not node:
                return
            inorder(node.left, acc)
            acc.append(node.val)
            inorder(node.right, acc)
        a = []
        inorder(root1, a)
        b = []
        inorder(root2, b)
        merged = []
        i = j = 0
        while i < len(a) and j < len(b):
            if a[i] <= b[j]:
                merged.append(a[i])
                i += 1
            else:
                merged.append(b[j])
                j += 1
        merged.extend(a[i:])
        merged.extend(b[j:])
        return merged
